title: Grimgrove - can you survive the dark and twisted forest? (Gamecrafter review)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ShkacKGq5Vk

Daniel (@dungeondive) is genuinely excited about Grimgrove, a Gamecrafter game crammed into a VHS clamshell box that somehow creates more dread than games ten times its size. The amateur art works *because* it's amateur, complemented by flavor text so atmospheric you can practically smell the cursed forest. You're trapped in an endless cycle, seven nights to escape, and failure comes swiftly when the tragedy snowballs.

Mechanically, energy cubes drawn from a bag determine your test colors, and you spend cubes to boost your dice pools or trigger trump colors for automatic success. It's elegantly tense—combat against spiders and reality warpers, fishing at lakes, trading with mushroom kids—each decision rippling into consequences. Daniel hasn't won yet, which says something about the difficulty curve. The game deliberately makes you feel like you're losing even when you're winning.

The real triumph is how it proves Games Workshop boxes were superior to modern cardboard—and how a modest rulebook, evocative writing, and genuine atmosphere can carry a solo crawl into your nightmares. At $40, it's a steal.

----

Do atmospheric horror and mechanical tension need to feed each other, or can one carry a game alone?
